finding.title: run_study emits phase/reasoning spans but never starts tracing or stamps run_id baggage — traces cannot be correlated to the run or the experiment-tracker run_id
finding.line: 209, 269-521 (phase spans via tracer.span); compare orchestration/graph.py:104-108 which does call bootstrap_tracing()+run_trace_context()
finding.description: run_study is the documented end-to-end composed runner and it opens trace spans for every phase (tracer.span('phase:INGEST') etc.) and the reasoning seam emits LLM/AGENT spans underneath. But run_study never calls bootstrap_tracing() and never wraps execution in run_trace_context(run_id) — grep of pipeline.py shows no reference to either. run_trace_context (tracing.py:145-163) is what stamps the 'assay.run_id' baggage onto every span so traces are correlatable across stores; the orchestration graph runner (graph.py:104-108) does exactly this, but the primary pipeline does not. Consequence: spans from a run_study execution carry no run_id baggage, so an operator cannot correlate the Phoenix trace for a given run with that run's experiment-tracker run_id (line 539 returns experiment_run_id) or its provenance trail — breaking end-to-end correlation, the explicitly-stated purpose of run_trace_context ("cross-store correlation"). Tracing also depends on something else having called bootstrap_tracing() first, which the composed entrypoint does not guarantee.
finding.suggested_fix: In run_study, after obtaining run_id (or a synthesized correlation id when no tracker is supplied), call bootstrap_tracing() once and wrap the phase machine in `with run_trace_context(corr_id):` so every phase and reasoning span is stamped with the run id and lands at the local collector — mirroring orchestration.graph.run(). Use the experiment_run_id when present so Phoenix spans and the MLflow run share one correlation key.

verdicts.reasoning:
The core mechanical claim is TRUE and verified. run_study (src/assay_engine/pipeline.py) opens phase spans via tracer.span("phase:INGEST") etc. (lines 271, 332, 360, 372, 406, 454, 486, 507) and the reasoning seam (src/assay_engine/reasoning/seam.py:473-490) emits LLM/AGENT spans under it, yet run_study NEVER calls bootstrap_tracing() and never wraps execution in run_trace_context(). A repo-wide grep confirms both symbols are referenced ONLY in src/assay_engine/orchestration/graph.py (lines 23, 105, 107, 133, 136); pipeline.py references neither. The comparison to graph.run (graph.py:104-108) is accurate, and the two are genuinely independent entrypoints — graph.run does NOT wrap run_study (no pipeline import in orchestration/). run_study is the documented, public, composed end-to-end runner (src/assay_engine/__init__.py:21,51,86 and ADR-0010 per its docstring), so the gap is on a primary entrypoint, not a dead path. My PoC confirms the baggage consequence: inside an OtelTracer span, baggage.get_baggage('assay.run_id') is None without run_trace_context and 'RUN-123' with it — so run_study's spans carry no run_id baggage.

I down-calibrate from MEDIUM to LOW for three concrete reasons. (1) It is never run-breaking: OtelTracer is a graceful no-op when no provider is registered, and run_trace_context is a no-op when run_id is None or OTel is absent — so the consequence is degraded observability, not a crash or wrong result. (2) StudyResult already returns experiment_run_id (pipeline.py:539) and the append-only provenance trail records run_start/phases independently, so a run is not un-correlatable in absolute terms — only the Phoenix-trace<->run_id link via baggage is missing. (3) Most importantly, I found that the correlation benefit the assessor attributes to run_trace_context is itself only partially wired even in the path it praises: there is no BaggageSpanProcessor installed anywhere (grep shows none) and phoenix.otel.register does not add one (inspected source: no 'baggage'/'BaggageSpanProcessor'). My second PoC shows that even inside run_trace_context('RUN-123'), the exported span attributes are just {'openinference.span.kind':'CHAIN'} — assay.run_id is NOT copied onto stored span attributes. So baggage helps in-process reads and cross-process OTLP propagation, but the "operator queries Phoenix spans by run_id" payoff the finding leans on is not fully realized even via graph.run. That overstatement, plus the graceful-degradation and the already-returned run_id, makes this a real-but-minor observability-completeness/consistency gap rather than MEDIUM.

finding.title: Module-global reasoning ThreadPoolExecutor is never shut down (no atexit), unlike the checkpointer pool
finding.description: `_pool = ThreadPoolExecutor(max_workers=8, ...)` is created at import time and has NO atexit shutdown and no graceful-cleanup path. This is exactly the leak the prompt flagged. The persistence layer treats this risk seriously: checkpoint.py registers a single shared `atexit.register(_close_all_pools)` (see checkpoint.py:148-154) precisely so worker threads are joined before interpreter finalization. The reasoning seam does not. PoC: `getattr(seam._pool,'_shutdown')` is `False` and `dir(seam)` contains no shutdown/atexit helper. Consequences: 8 daemon-ish worker threads persist for the process lifetime; at interpreter shutdown the pool can be torn down implicitly while a worker is still blocked in a backend call, and any code that imports the seam in a short-lived worker/subprocess accumulates threads with no deterministic join. Inconsistent with the codebase's own hardening standard for the Postgres pool.
finding.suggested_fix: Register a single idempotent atexit handler that calls `_pool.shutdown(wait=False, cancel_futures=True)` (mirroring checkpoint.py's `_register_pool_cleanup`/`_close_all_pools` pattern), or lazily construct the pool and expose an explicit `shutdown()` for embedders. Guard against double-registration with a module flag like checkpoint.py's `_atexit_registered`.

verdicts.reasoning:
The finding's literal facts are correct (the module-global ThreadPoolExecutor at seam.py:136 has no explicit atexit/shutdown helper, and checkpoint.py:143-154 does register one), but the asserted consequences — thread leak, no deterministic join, accumulation in short-lived workers, inconsistency with the codebase standard — do not hold, so this is not a production-readiness defect.

1. No leak / deterministic join already exists. The CPython stdlib module concurrent.futures.thread registers its OWN process-wide atexit handler (_python_exit) which, for EVERY ThreadPoolExecutor instance, places a sentinel on each worker queue and t.join()s every worker thread at interpreter shutdown. I confirmed via PoC that the reasoning pool's worker (reasoning-timeout_0) registers in concurrent.futures.thread._threads_queues, meaning it is joined automatically at exit with no per-pool atexit needed. The explicit atexit the finding wants is redundant for a vanilla ThreadPoolExecutor.

2. The checkpoint.py analogy is invalid. checkpoint.py's atexit calls pool.close() on psycopg ConnectionPool objects — i.e. live Postgres network connections the stdlib knows nothing about, which genuinely require explicit teardown. The reasoning _pool is a plain stdlib ThreadPoolExecutor, already covered by the stdlib's join handler. Different resource types; the 'inconsistent with the codebase's own standard' claim conflates them.

3. 'Short-lived worker/subprocess accumulates threads' is false: the module is import-cached (I confirmed re-import yields the same _pool object), so there is exactly one pool per process, capped at 8 workers, joined at that process's exit.

4. Workers are created lazily — at import there are zero reasoning threads (only MainThread); they spawn on first submit, bounded to 8, with a saturation guard (_submit_bounded). The 'torn down while a worker is blocked in a backend call' risk is identical with or without an explicit atexit, and the code already documents (lines 174-177) that a ThreadPoolExecutor cannot interrupt a thread blocked in a backend call; the suggested cancel_futures=True only cancels not-yet-started futures and cannot interrupt an in-flight blocked call either, so it would not change behavior for the scenario described.

The suggested fix (explicit shutdown() for embedders) is a minor library-ergonomics nicety, not a reliability/resource-management defect. The leak it claims to fix does not occur.
verdicts.evidence:
Read src/assay_engine/reasoning/seam.py (lines 1-576; pool at 132-177) and src/assay_engine/persistence/checkpoint.py (133-168, plus grep of atexit/_OPEN_POOLS/_atexit_registered at 30,39,63-64,144,149-154).

PoC #1 (venv): import seam → _pool type ThreadPoolExecutor, _pool._shutdown=False, no shutdown/atexit/cleanup helper in dir(seam), active threads at import = ['MainThread'] (lazy worker creation, zero threads at import).

PoC #2 (venv): inspect.getsource(concurrent.futures.thread._python_exit) shows it iterates _threads_queues, puts sentinel None on each, then t.join()s every worker — a stdlib-registered atexit join covering all ThreadPoolExecutors.

PoC #3 (venv): after seam._submit_bounded(lambda:42), concurrent.futures.thread._threads_queues has the reasoning worker registered (len 1), reasoning-timeout_0 alive → it WILL be joined by stdlib _python_exit at exit. So a deterministic join already happens.

PoC #4 (venv): importlib re-import of the module returns the same _pool object (import-cached → one pool per process), _POOL_WORKERS=8 cap — refuting the 'short-lived worker accumulates threads' claim.

grep confirms reasoning/ has zero atexit references and checkpoint.py's atexit closes psycopg ConnectionPool objects (pool.close()), a distinct resource the stdlib does not manage.

verdicts.reasoning:
The factual surface of the finding is accurate (confirmed via PoC): src/assay_engine/reasoning/seam.py:136 creates a module-global ThreadPoolExecutor(max_workers=8) at import, with no atexit handler and no shutdown/cleanup helper (seam._pool._shutdown is False; dir(seam) has no cleanup helpers). But the claimed CONSEQUENCES are wrong, making this a false positive.

1) No thread leak at interpreter exit. Python's concurrent.futures.thread module registers its OWN atexit handler (_python_exit) at import. ThreadPoolExecutor worker threads are non-daemon and registered in _threads_queues; at shutdown _python_exit enqueues a sentinel to every pool and joins() every worker. I read the stdlib source to confirm. Idle workers are therefore cleanly joined automatically — the seam does not need its own atexit hook to avoid leaking threads.

2) The "pool torn down while a worker is blocked in a backend call" scenario is backwards. Because workers are non-daemon and joined by _python_exit, a blocked worker would BLOCK interpreter shutdown until its call returns — it is not abruptly torn down. Moreover the suggested fix shutdown(wait=False, cancel_futures=True) would NOT help: cancel_futures only cancels not-yet-started futures; a future already running in a worker cannot be cancelled/interrupted. The code's own comment (lines 174-177) correctly documents exactly this ThreadPoolExecutor limitation.

3) The checkpoint.py analogy is false. psycopg ConnectionPool holds OS-level DB sockets/connections that NO stdlib atexit hook cleans up — that is precisely why checkpoint.py must register _close_all_pools. A stdlib ThreadPoolExecutor is categorically different: the interpreter already cleans it. Holding the seam to the "same standard" conflates two unrelated resource types.

4) No accumulation in subprocesses/short-lived workers. The pool is a single module-global (max 8 threads per process), not per-instance; each process has exactly one and joins it at exit. Nothing accumulates across instances.

A marginal nicety (an explicit shutdown() for in-process embedders) exists, but with a single process-global capped pool there is no leak, no accumulation, and no unsafe teardown. This is not a production-readiness defect.
verdicts.evidence: Read src/assay_engine/reasoning/seam.py (full): line 136 `_pool = ThreadPoolExecutor(max_workers=_POOL_WORKERS, thread_name_prefix="reasoning-timeout")`, default (non-daemon) threads; saturation guard in _submit_bounded; _with_timeout comment lines 174-177 documents that a ThreadPoolExecutor cannot interrupt a thread blocked in a backend call. Read checkpoint.py:133-155 confirming its atexit is for psycopg ConnectionPool (OS sockets). PoC in venv: `seam._pool._shutdown` -> False; no cleanup helpers in dir(seam); confirmed concurrent.futures.thread defines _python_exit and _threads_queues. Read stdlib _python_exit source: it sets _shutdown, puts sentinel None on every pool's queue, and t.join()s every worker — i.e. stdlib already joins reasoning pool workers at interpreter exit. _adjust_thread_count creates threading.Thread workers (non-daemon by default), registered for that join.
